Researcher Track · Molecular Evolution · Selection Inference

Selection on sexual dichromatism in primates lands on both halves of one coupled network: a melanoblast-development module and a set of steroid-metabolism enzymes.

Across four genomically-sampled independent origins of pigment dichromatism, HyPhy RELAX identifies nine BH-significant, certified genes on the dichromatic foreground — eight under intensified selection, three in pigmentation (TFAP2A, KITLG, EDN3) and five in sex-hormone metabolism (HSD17B1, HSD17B12, HSD17B7, CYP7B1, SRD5A1), plus one hormone-set gene, SHBG, under significant relaxation — while MC1R, the single-gene driver of the analogous bird phenotype, does not clear correction. Two of the nine (EDN3, SRD5A1) carry Tier-B QC flags; seven are unconditionally clean.

117–238-species primate phylogeny · HyPhy RELAX, Benjamini–Hochberg correction, alignment/branch-length QC gate · companion aBSREL / RERconverge layers

Design · 01

The unit of the test is the independent origin, not the dichromatic species

Dichromatism was scored across a 238-species primate table (93 monochromatic; 24 coded dichromatic — 9 complete, 15 partial). Pooling those 24 tips into one foreground presupposes a single shared architecture, which is the question under test: parsimony reconstruction on the full tree resolves them into 15 independent phenotypic origins, and 42 percent of the pooled 24-tip foreground is redundant within-clade signal from a small number of multi-tip radiations.

Scale note. The full-tree reconstruction counts ~14–15 phenotypic origins of dichromatism. The selection test below is powered only on the 4 clades with usable genomic sampling (Lemuridae/Eulemur, Cebidae, Hylobatidae, Cercopithecidae) — per-origin RELAX requires ≥3 foreground tips, and only 3 of those 4 clades reach it. Cite the 4 tested clades, not the 14–15 phenotypic count, when describing what selection was actually inferred on.
Result · 02

Nine genes clear BH correction and QC; eight nominal hits do not

HyPhy RELAX tests K > 1 (intensified) / K < 1 (relaxed) selection on the dichromatic foreground per gene, Benjamini–Hochberg corrected, behind a QC gate on alignment coverage, branch length, K bounds, and foreground n. Of 17 genes nominally BH-significant, 9 pass QC and are certified as hits (7 unconditionally clean, 2 with disclosed Tier-B flags); 8 are alignment artifacts and are not reported as hits.

GeneModuleKpBHQC
TFAP2Apigmentation1.931×10⁻⁴clean
KITLGpigmentation1.301.5×10⁻³clean
EDN3pigmentation1.281.5×10⁻³branch-length flag
HSD17B1hormone2.93<1×10⁻³clean
HSD17B12hormone2.52<1×10⁻³clean
CYP7B1hormone1.377.8×10⁻³clean
HSD17B7hormone1.324.0×10⁻²clean
SRD5A1hormone2.32<1×10⁻³gaps>20% flag
SHBGhormone (relaxed)0.242.9×10⁻²clean
POMC—3.1—fails QC (aln 0.68)
TYR—2.2—fails QC (aln 0.65)
Limitation. POMC (K=3.1) and TYR (K=2.2) show strongly intensified nominal signal and are the biologically obvious pigment–hormone bridge (α-MSH + ACTH), but their alignments are truncated extractions (reference-length ratio 0.68 / 0.65) and are not certified. A denser genome panel is a stated future direction, not a reported result. SHBG is the one significant relaxation among the nine certified genes and is a carrier/transport gene, not a biosynthetic enzyme — it is not intensified.
Result · 03 · Hero

The certified hits are not a scattered gene list — they resolve into two connected modules

Panel A places the three certified pigmentation hits on the melanoblast-development network: TFAP2A, KITLG, and EDN3 sit upstream of the SOX10/MITF convergence node that the whole pigmentation network is wired through. Panel B places the six certified hormone hits on a full steroid-axis map, by biological sub-axis (steroidogenesis, androgen, estrogen, HPG axis, GnRH signalling, carriers): the certified genes cluster in steroid-biosynthesis enzymes (HSD17B1/7/12, CYP7B1, SRD5A1) plus the one relaxed carrier gene (SHBG), not in nuclear receptors (AR, ESR1/2 are fitted but not significant) or in the upstream HPG axis.

The set-level comparison is not significant. A 100,000-permutation label-swap test on whether the pigmentation set or the hormone set carries more selection signal overall gives p≈0.47; a Wilcoxon rank-sum test on the same contrast gives p≈0.11. Neither gene set dominates the other. The claim here is about architecture — selection lands on genes drawn from two distinct, biologically coherent modules within one coupled network — not that one module carries more signal than the other. Do not read this figure as "hormones beat pigment" or the reverse.
Result · 04 · Cross-taxon comparison

The bird single-gene switch does not carry over to primates

In galliform birds, sexual dichromatism tracks dN/dS at one locus, MC1R (Nadeau et al. 2007, Proc. R. Soc. B, doi:10.1098/rspb.2007.0174). In this primate panel MC1R is QC-clean but shows only a weak, non-significant shift toward relaxation: K=0.45, uncorrected p=0.043, pBH=0.144. It does not clear multiple-testing correction and is not reported as a hit.

Careful framing. The correct statement is "the bird's single-gene switch is silent in primates" — MC1R is not a certified hit in either direction. It is not correct to say "MC1R is under relaxation in primates," since that value does not clear significance after correction. The primate signal is not a shift from one gene to another single gene; it is a shift from a single-locus architecture to a polygenic one spanning two functional modules.
Synthesis

One coupled network, read three ways

The pigmentation and sex-hormone gene sets are not two independent lists that happen to both show selection. They are physically wired together at a small number of nodes — MC1R, GPER1, AR, ESR2 — that sit inside the melanocortin and estrogen signalling arms and converge on the same MITF/TYR pigment-synthesis machinery. The primate selection result above is the deep-time reading of that coupling. Two further, independently-sourced readings of the same network — human disease architecture and pharmacology — are shown below as subordinate, connective material: they do not add new selection evidence, but they show that the network drawn from the selection data recurs in domains the selection analysis never touched.

Reading 1 — deep time (this project's certified result, above). Selection acts on genes from both modules across independent origins of primate dichromatism: TFAP2A/KITLG/EDN3 intensified in pigmentation; HSD17B1/7/12/CYP7B1/SRD5A1 intensified and SHBG relaxed in steroid metabolism.
Synthesis, continued

Reading 2 (human disease) and reading 3 (pharmacology) of the same network

A curated set of 200 human pigmentation-associated gene–disease entries (OMIM-style classification; companion table, not part of the primate selection pipeline) separates by mechanism. Structural and enzymatic melanocyte-autonomous genes (melanosome biogenesis, melanin synthesis, MITF dosage; n=29) are overwhelmingly one-directional — 27 give hypopigmentation, only 2 give hyperpigmentation — and the effect is patchy or localized in nearly every case. The seven genes acting through the circulating ACTH/MSH axis split far more evenly (6 hyper, 1 hypo) and are always generalized, whole-body.

Reading 2 — human disease. The diffusible-hormone class of the network is far more bidirectional than the structural class: 6 of 7 hormone-axis genes give hyperpigmentation and 1 gives hypopigmentation, versus 2 of 29 structural genes giving hyperpigmentation. A structural gene's effect is largely fixed by what the protein does; a hormone-axis gene's effect depends on which way the ligand is dialed — consistent with it being a systemic control point rather than a local structural component.
Reading 3 — pharmacology. Afamelanotide, an approved synthetic α-MSH analog (marketed as Scenesse, for erythropoietic protoporphyria), and the related unapproved analog Melanotan-II, both act as exogenous agonists at the same melanocortin receptor and produce generalized skin darkening. That a single diffusible ligand analog reproduces the whole-body phenotype is literature-established pharmacology, cited here as illustrative of the same mechanistic logic as Reading 2 — it is not a result of this project's selection or disease-classification pipelines.
Scope of this synthesis. Readings 2 and 3 are drawn from a companion human-disease classification table and established pharmacology literature, respectively. They support the same coupled- network architecture that the primate RELAX result identifies in Reading 1, but they are not new selection evidence and should not be conflated with the certified primate hits above.
Limitations, consolidated

What this result does and does not show

  • Set-level contrast is not significant (permutation p≈0.47; Wilcoxon p≈0.11). The claim is architecture — genes from both modules are selected — not that one module dominates.
  • MC1R is not a certified hit (K=0.45, pBH=0.144). It is silent, not significantly relaxed.
  • POMC and TYR fail alignment QC (reference-length ratio 0.68 / 0.65) and are excluded from all hit counts, despite favorable nominal K values.
  • The selection test is powered on 4 genomically-sampled clades, not the ~14–15 phenotypic origins visible on the full 238-species tree; per-origin RELAX itself is only well-powered for 3 of those 4 (≥2 tips each).
  • EDN3 and SRD5A1 carry QC flags (branch-length and alignment-gap respectively) and are reported as Tier-B, rescue-pending, not unconditionally clean; 7 of the 9 certified genes are.
  • SHBG is relaxed, not intensified (K=0.24) — the one significant relaxation among the nine certified genes, and a carrier/transport gene rather than a biosynthetic enzyme.
  • Readings 2 and 3 (human disease, pharmacology) are corroborating context from outside the selection pipeline, not additional statistical evidence for the primate result.
Researcher Track · Rubric

Three questions, answered plainly

What did Claude Science help do?
Ran a QC-gated HyPhy RELAX selection screen across a 117–238-species primate panel at the level of independent phenotypic origins rather than pooled species, separated 9 certified hits from 8 alignment artifacts among 17 nominally significant genes, and assembled the pigmentation/hormone network figures directly from the certified table.
How are we closer to a finding?
The genomic architecture of primate sexual dichromatism is now characterized, for the first time at clade scale, as polygenic and modular — split between a melanoblast-development module and a steroid-metabolism enzyme set — rather than resting on the single MC1R locus that explains the analogous bird phenotype. Which module carries more of the signal remains statistically undetermined.
What is the flagship?
Certified selection on both halves of one coupled pigment–hormone network in independently-evolved dichromatic primate lineages (Figure 2 above), read alongside two subordinate, honest corroborations of the same network architecture in human disease classification and pharmacology.
v3-certified RELAX run · BH correction · QC gate: pct_gaps, K bounds, n_foreground≥3, tree_len, aln_ref_ratio∈[0.8,1.2] · companion aBSREL / RERconverge layers · human disease and pharmacology material subordinate, not counted as selection evidence
